question_text: Which type of isomerism is shown by [Co(NH₃)₅Br]SO₄ and [Co(NH₃)₅SO₄]Br?
explanation: These two complexes are ionization isomers. In ionization isomerism, the ligand and the counter ion exchange places. In the first complex, Br⁻ is a ligand and SO₄²⁻ is the counter ion, while in the second complex, SO₄²⁻ acts as a ligand and Br⁻ is the counter ion. These isomers produce different ions in solution and thus give different reactions with reagents like AgNO₃ or BaCl₂.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: Linkage isomerism involves an ambidentate ligand bonding through different donor atoms, not an exchange of ligand and counter ion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing linkage isomerism with ionization isomerism.

elimination_hints.hint: Coordination isomerism occurs in compounds containing both cationic and anionic complex ions where ligands are exchanged between the two.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing coordination isomerism with ionization isomerism; here only one complex ion exists.

elimination_hints.hint: Geometrical isomerism is a type of stereoisomerism involving spatial arrangement, not a change in the composition of the coordination sphere.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up structural and stereoisomerism.

question_text: Which of the following ligands can exhibit linkage isomerism?
explanation: The nitrite ion (NO₂⁻) is an ambidentate ligand, meaning it can bind to the central metal atom through either of two different donor atoms. It can coordinate through the nitrogen atom (as nitro, –NO₂) or through the oxygen atom (as nitrito, –ONO). This ability to bind through different atoms makes it capable of exhibiting linkage isomerism. CN⁻ and NH₃ are not ambidentate, and Cl⁻ is a monodentate ligand with only one donor atom.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: CN⁻ typically coordinates only through carbon in most complexes and is not classified as an ambidentate ligand for NEET purposes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all polyatomic ligands are ambidentate.

elimination_hints.hint: NH₃ is a monodentate ligand that coordinates only through nitrogen; it has only one donor atom.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing monodentate neutral ligands with ambidentate ligands.

elimination_hints.hint: Cl⁻ is a monodentate ligand with a single donor atom and cannot exhibit linkage isomerism.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking halide ions can show linkage isomerism.

question_text: The complex [Pt(NH₃)₂Cl₂] with a square planar geometry can exhibit which type of stereoisomerism?
explanation: The square planar complex [Pt(NH₃)₂Cl₂] with the general formula [Ma₂b₂] can show geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism. In the cis isomer, the two identical ligands are adjacent to each other (at 90°), while in the trans isomer, they are positioned opposite to each other (at 180°). This is a classic example of geometrical isomerism frequently asked in NEET examinations.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: Square planar complexes of the type [Ma₂b₂] generally do not show optical isomerism because they possess a plane of symmetry.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing geometrical isomerism with optical isomerism in planar complexes.

elimination_hints.hint: Linkage isomerism requires ambidentate ligands like NO₂⁻ or SCN⁻; NH₃ and Cl⁻ are not ambidentate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing that linkage isomerism is a structural isomerism involving ambidentate ligands.

elimination_hints.hint: Ionization isomerism involves exchange between a ligand and counter ion, which is not the case in this uncharged complex.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing stereoisomerism with structural isomerism.

question_text: Which of the following complexes represents a hydrate isomer of [Cr(H₂O)₆]Cl₃?
explanation: Hydrate (solvate) isomerism arises when water molecules can either be directly bonded to the metal ion as ligands in the coordination sphere or remain outside as water of crystallization. [Cr(H₂O)₆]Cl₃ is the violet-colored isomer where all six water molecules are coordinated. [Cr(H₂O)₅Cl]Cl₂·H₂O is its hydrate isomer, where one Cl⁻ has entered the coordination sphere replacing a water molecule, which then exists as lattice water.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: This complex contains NH₃ ligands instead of H₂O, so it cannot be a hydrate isomer of the given complex.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing hydrate isomerism with substitution of a completely different ligand.

elimination_hints.hint: This complex has Br⁻ as the counter ion instead of Cl⁻, making it a different compound entirely.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring that isomers must have the same molecular formula.

elimination_hints.hint: This complex has ethylenediamine (en) ligands, giving it a completely different molecular formula.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing that isomers must share the same molecular formula.

question_text: Coordination isomerism is possible in which of the following compounds?
explanation: Coordination isomerism occurs in compounds that contain both cationic and anionic complex ions. In such compounds, the ligands can be exchanged between the two complex ions. [Co(NH₃)₆][Cr(CN)₆] is such a compound — Co³⁺ forms the cationic complex and Cr³⁺ forms the anionic complex. Its coordination isomer would be [Cr(NH₃)₆][Co(CN)₆], where the ligands are exchanged between the two metals. The other options either have only one complex ion or no complex ion at all.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: This compound has only one complex ion [Co(NH₃)₅Cl]²⁺ with simple Cl⁻ counter ions; coordination isomerism needs two complex ions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking ionization isomerism and coordination isomerism are the same.

elimination_hints.hint: This is a neutral complex with no counter ions, so coordination isomerism is not possible.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming any complex with multiple ligands can show coordination isomerism.

elimination_hints.hint: This compound has only one complex anion [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ with simple K⁺ ions, not a second complex ion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a simple metal cation with a complex cation.

question_text: When SCN⁻ acts as a ligand, it can bond through S or N. The isomer in which SCN⁻ binds through nitrogen is called:
explanation: SCN⁻ (thiocyanate ion) is an ambidentate ligand that can coordinate through either the sulfur atom or the nitrogen atom. When it bonds through the sulfur atom, it is called thiocyanato (S-bonded), and when it bonds through the nitrogen atom, it is called isothiocyanato (N-bonded). The prefix 'iso' is used to denote bonding through the less common donor atom. This difference in bonding leads to linkage isomerism in coordination compounds.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: Thiocyanato refers to bonding through the sulfur atom (S-bonded SCN⁻), not through nitrogen.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the naming convention for S-bonded and N-bonded thiocyanate.

elimination_hints.hint: Cyanato refers to the OCN⁻ (cyanate) ion, which is a different ligand from SCN⁻ (thiocyanate).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cyanate (OCN⁻) with thiocyanate (SCN⁻).

elimination_hints.hint: Nitrito refers to the O-bonded form of the NO₂⁻ ligand (–ONO), which is an entirely different ambidentate ligand.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up the naming of different ambidentate ligands.

question_text: Which of the following octahedral complexes is optically active?
explanation: [Co(en)₃]³⁺ is optically active because it lacks a plane of symmetry and exists as non-superimposable mirror images (enantiomers). The three bidentate ethylenediamine (en) ligands wrap around the octahedral cobalt ion in a helical arrangement, creating chirality. The complex exists as Δ (delta, right-handed) and Λ (lambda, left-handed) enantiomers. In contrast, trans-[Co(en)₂Cl₂]⁺ has a plane of symmetry, and [Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺ is highly symmetrical with no chirality.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: The trans isomer of [Co(en)₂Cl₂]⁺ possesses a plane of symmetry and is therefore optically inactive. Only its cis form is optically active.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that trans isomers of [Ma₂b₂] octahedral type have a symmetry plane.

elimination_hints.hint: [Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺ has all identical monodentate ligands, giving it a high degree of symmetry (Oh) with multiple planes and axes of symmetry.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all octahedral complexes with bidentate-like arrangements are chiral.

elimination_hints.hint: The trans form of [CoCl₂(NH₃)₄]⁺ has a centre of symmetry and a plane of symmetry, making it optically inactive.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing symmetry elements in trans complexes.

question_text: An octahedral complex of the type [Ma₃b₃] can show which of the following types of isomerism?
explanation: An octahedral complex of the type [Ma₃b₃] can exhibit facial (fac) and meridional (mer) isomerism. In the fac isomer, three identical ligands occupy one face of the octahedron (i.e., they are on the same triangular face), while in the mer isomer, the three identical ligands are arranged in a plane that includes the central metal atom (meridian). These are both geometrical isomers. This type of isomerism is distinct from cis-trans isomerism seen in [Ma₂b₂] type complexes.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: Cis-trans isomerism in octahedral complexes is observed for [Ma₂b₄] or [Ma₂b₂c₂] types, not for [Ma₃b₃].
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying cis-trans terminology to complexes where fac-mer description is appropriate.

elimination_hints.hint: Ionization isomerism depends on the exchange of a ligand with a counter ion and is unrelated to the spatial arrangement of ligands.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing structural isomerism with stereoisomerism.

elimination_hints.hint: Linkage isomerism requires the presence of ambidentate ligands and does not depend on the arrangement of ligands around the metal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between conditions for different types of isomerism.

question_text: Which of the following conditions is necessary for a coordination compound to exhibit geometrical isomerism?
explanation: For a coordination compound to exhibit geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism, there must be at least two different types of ligands present and the geometry must allow for distinct spatial arrangements. In square planar complexes, the formula must be of the type [Ma₂b₂] or [Mabcd], and in octahedral complexes, types like [Ma₂b₄], [Ma₂b₂c₂], etc., can show geometrical isomerism. Tetrahedral complexes cannot show geometrical isomerism because all positions are equivalent with respect to each other.
subject: chemistry

options.text: The complex must have two or more types of ligands in a square planar or octahedral geometry
The complex must have all identical ligands
The complex must have a tetrahedral geometry
The complex must contain only monodentate ligands

elimination_hints.hint: If all ligands are identical, such as in [Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺, there is only one possible spatial arrangement and no geometrical isomers can exist.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not realizing that at least two different ligand types are needed for distinct spatial arrangements.

elimination_hints.hint: Tetrahedral complexes do not exhibit geometrical isomerism because all four positions around the central atom are equivalent to each other.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all geometries can show cis-trans isomerism.

elimination_hints.hint: Bidentate ligands like en can also give rise to geometrical isomerism, as seen in cis and trans [Co(en)₂Cl₂]⁺.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking geometrical isomerism is limited to complexes with only monodentate ligands.

question_text: How many moles of AgCl will be precipitated when one mole of [Co(NH₃)₅Cl]Cl₂ is treated with excess AgNO₃?
explanation: In the complex [Co(NH₃)₅Cl]Cl₂, one Cl⁻ is present inside the coordination sphere as a ligand and two Cl⁻ ions are present outside as counter ions. Only the ions outside the coordination sphere (ionizable ions) can react with AgNO₃ to form a precipitate. Therefore, when treated with excess AgNO₃, only the two ionic Cl⁻ ions outside the coordination sphere will react to give 2 moles of AgCl precipitate. The Cl⁻ bonded to Co³⁺ as a ligand will not be precipitated.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: Only 1 of the 3 Cl⁻ ions is inside the coordination sphere as a ligand; the remaining 2 are ionizable counter ions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Counting only one of the two counter ions as free.

elimination_hints.hint: Not all three Cl⁻ ions are free; one is a coordinated ligand bound to the metal and cannot react with AgNO₃.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all chloride ions in the formula are ionizable.

elimination_hints.hint: The two Cl⁻ outside the coordination sphere dissociate in solution and are free to react with AgNO₃.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking all chloride ions are bound to the metal as ligands.

question_text: How many geometrical isomers are possible for the octahedral complex [Co(NH₃)₃Cl₃]?
explanation: The octahedral complex [Co(NH₃)₃Cl₃] is of the type [Ma₃b₃] and can exhibit two geometrical isomers: facial (fac) and meridional (mer). In the facial isomer, the three identical ligands (e.g., three Cl⁻) occupy three corners of one triangular face of the octahedron, making mutual angles of 90°. In the meridional isomer, the three identical ligands lie in a plane passing through the metal centre (meridian), with two of them trans to each other (180°) and the third cis to both (90°).
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: With two different types of ligands in a 3:3 ratio around an octahedral centre, more than one spatial arrangement is possible.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming only one arrangement is possible for any complex.

elimination_hints.hint: Three geometrical isomers are not possible for the [Ma₃b₃] type; careful analysis shows only fac and mer forms exist.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overcounting by treating equivalent arrangements as different.

elimination_hints.hint: Four isomers exceed the maximum number possible for [Ma₃b₃]; systematic enumeration gives only two distinct arrangements.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing [Ma₃b₃] with complexes having more varied ligand sets like [Mabcdef].

question_text: Which of the following statements about the cis and trans isomers of [PtCl₂(NH₃)₂] is correct?
explanation: The cis isomer of [PtCl₂(NH₃)₂] (cisplatin) is widely used as an anticancer drug, while the trans isomer (transplatin) is therapeutically inactive. Cisplatin works by cross-linking adjacent purine bases on DNA, disrupting DNA replication in cancer cells. The cis isomer has a non-zero dipole moment because the bond dipoles of the two Cl–Pt bonds do not cancel each other, while in the trans isomer, the bond dipoles cancel out, giving a zero dipole moment. This difference in dipole moment can be used to distinguish between the two isomers.
subject: chemistry

options.text: The trans isomer is used as an anticancer drug
Both isomers have zero dipole moment
Both isomers are equally effective as anticancer drugs
The cis isomer has a non-zero dipole moment and is used as an anticancer drug

elimination_hints.hint: It is the cis isomer (cisplatin) that is the well-known anticancer drug; the trans isomer is therapeutically inactive.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing which geometrical isomer of [PtCl₂(NH₃)₂] has anticancer activity.

elimination_hints.hint: Only the trans isomer has zero dipole moment due to cancellation of bond dipoles; the cis isomer has a net dipole moment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not considering the vector sum of bond dipoles in different geometrical arrangements.

elimination_hints.hint: The anticancer activity is specific to the cis isomer; the trans isomer does not show the same biological activity due to its different geometry.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming isomers with the same molecular formula always have the same biological properties.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Tetrahedral complexes do not show geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism.
Reason (R): In a tetrahedral geometry, all the four positions are equivalent with respect to each other.
explanation: The assertion is correct — tetrahedral complexes do not exhibit geometrical isomerism. The reason is also correct and properly explains the assertion. In a regular tetrahedron, every pair of ligand positions has the same angular relationship (bond angle approximately 109.5°). There is no distinction between 'adjacent' and 'opposite' positions as there is in square planar or octahedral geometries. Since there is no possibility of different relative spatial arrangements, cis-trans isomerism cannot arise in tetrahedral complexes.
subject: chemistry

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The equivalence of all positions in a tetrahedron is exactly the reason why cis-trans isomerism is absent; thus R correctly explains A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to connect positional equivalence with the impossibility of different spatial arrangements.

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct — in a tetrahedron, all four positions are indeed equivalent, with the same bond angle between any two.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly believing that tetrahedral positions are non-equivalent.

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is true; tetrahedral complexes genuinely do not exhibit cis-trans isomerism, as confirmed by NCERT.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly assuming tetrahedral complexes can show geometrical isomerism.

question_text:
Assertion (A): [Co(en)₃]³⁺ is optically active.
Reason (R): The complex [Co(en)₃]³⁺ lacks a plane of symmetry and exists as non-superimposable mirror images.
explanation: The assertion is correct — [Co(en)₃]³⁺ is indeed optically active. The reason is also correct and is the correct explanation. The tris(ethylenediamine)cobalt(III) ion has three bidentate en ligands arranged around the octahedral Co³⁺ ion in a propeller-like fashion. This arrangement creates a chiral complex that lacks any plane of symmetry or centre of inversion. Its mirror image cannot be superimposed on the original, resulting in two enantiomers designated as Δ (delta) and Λ (lambda) forms, which rotate the plane of polarized light in opposite directions.
subject: chemistry

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The absence of a plane of symmetry and the existence of non-superimposable mirror images is precisely the condition for optical activity; R directly explains A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing that chirality (absence of symmetry elements) is the fundamental cause of optical activity.

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct; [Co(en)₃]³⁺ indeed lacks a plane of symmetry and its mirror image is non-superimposable.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly thinking that octahedral complexes with bidentate ligands always possess a symmetry plane.

elimination_hints.hint: [Co(en)₃]³⁺ is a well-established example of an optically active coordination compound found in NCERT.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing optical activity with geometrical isomerism or assuming only carbon compounds are optically active.

question_text:
Match the isomer pairs in Column I with the type of isomerism in Column II.

Column I:
(P) [Co(NH₃)₅(NO₂)]²⁺ and [Co(NH₃)₅(ONO)]²⁺
(Q) [Co(NH₃)₅Br]SO₄ and [Co(NH₃)₅SO₄]Br
(R) [Cr(H₂O)₆]Cl₃ and [Cr(H₂O)₅Cl]Cl₂·H₂O
(S) [Co(en)₃][Cr(ox)₃] and [Cr(en)₃][Co(ox)₃]

Column II:
(1) Ionization isomerism
(2) Linkage isomerism
(3) Hydrate isomerism
(4) Coordination isomerism
explanation: (P) matches with (2): NO₂⁻ is an ambidentate ligand that binds through N (nitro) or O (nitrito), giving linkage isomers. (Q) matches with (1): Br⁻ and SO₄²⁻ exchange between the coordination sphere and counter ion, which is ionization isomerism. (R) matches with (3): H₂O and Cl⁻ exchange between the coordination sphere and outside (lattice water), which is hydrate (solvate) isomerism. (S) matches with (4): The ligands en and ox are exchanged between the two complex ions [Co]³⁺ and [Cr]³⁺, which is coordination isomerism.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: P involves the ambidentate ligand NO₂⁻ bonding through different atoms, which is linkage isomerism (2), not ionization isomerism (1).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ionization isomerism with linkage isomerism.

elimination_hints.hint: Q involves exchange of Br⁻ and SO₄²⁻ between coordination sphere and counter ion, which is ionization isomerism (1), not hydrate isomerism (3).
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up ionization and hydrate isomerism.

elimination_hints.hint: P involves NO₂⁻ binding through different donor atoms, which is linkage isomerism (2), not coordination isomerism (4).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing linkage isomerism with coordination isomerism.

question_text:
Match the coordination compounds in Column I with the type(s) of stereoisomerism they can exhibit in Column II.

Column I:
(P) [Pt(NH₃)₂Cl₂] (square planar)
(Q) [Co(en)₃]³⁺ (octahedral)
(R) [CoCl₂(en)₂]⁺ (octahedral)
(S) [Ni(CO)₄] (tetrahedral)

Column II:
(1) Geometrical isomerism only
(2) Optical isomerism only
(3) Both geometrical and optical isomerism
(4) Neither geometrical nor optical isomerism
explanation: (P) [Pt(NH₃)₂Cl₂] is a square planar [Ma₂b₂] complex that shows cis-trans (geometrical) isomerism only; it does not show optical isomerism as both isomers have a plane of symmetry. (Q) [Co(en)₃]³⁺ has all identical bidentate ligands, so no geometrical isomers exist, but it is chiral and shows optical isomerism only. (R) [CoCl₂(en)₂]⁺ shows both: cis and trans geometrical isomers, and the cis form further exists as optical isomers (Δ and Λ). (S) [Ni(CO)₄] is a tetrahedral complex with all identical ligands; it shows neither geometrical nor optical isomerism.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: Q ([Co(en)₃]³⁺) has all identical ligands so it cannot show geometrical isomerism; it shows only optical isomerism (2), not both (3).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the presence of bidentate ligands with geometrical isomerism.

elimination_hints.hint: P ([Pt(NH₃)₂Cl₂]) is a planar complex with a plane of symmetry in both cis and trans forms, so it does not show optical isomerism; it shows geometrical isomerism only (1), not both (3).
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all complexes with cis-trans forms also show optical activity.

elimination_hints.hint: R ([CoCl₂(en)₂]⁺) shows both geometrical and optical isomerism (3), not just geometrical (1); the cis form of this complex is optically active.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overlooking that the cis isomer of [CoCl₂(en)₂]⁺ lacks a plane of symmetry and is chiral.

question_text: Linkage isomerism arises in coordination compounds that contain ambidentate ligands.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Linkage isomerism is a type of structural isomerism that arises when a ligand can coordinate to the metal through two different donor atoms. Such ligands are called ambidentate ligands. Common examples include NO₂⁻ (which can bind through N as nitro or through O as nitrito) and SCN⁻ (which can bind through S as thiocyanato or through N as isothiocyanato). The presence of an ambidentate ligand is the essential requirement for linkage isomerism.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is factually correct as per NCERT; linkage isomerism specifically requires ambidentate ligands that can bond through different atoms.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing linkage isomerism with other types of structural isomerism.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable for a True/False question.
This option is not applicable for a True/False question.

question_text: Tetrahedral complexes of the type [Mabcd] show geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. Tetrahedral complexes do not show geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism regardless of the number of different ligands. This is because in a regular tetrahedron, all four positions are equivalent with respect to each other — there is no distinction between adjacent and opposite positions as in square planar or octahedral geometries. However, tetrahedral complexes of the type [Mabcd] with four different ligands can show optical isomerism because they lack a plane of symmetry.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: Tetrahedral complexes never show cis-trans isomerism because all positions are equivalent; the statement is false.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming that having different ligands is sufficient for geometrical isomerism, ignoring geometry constraints.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable for a True/False question.
This option is not applicable for a True/False question.

question_text: The complex [Co(NH₃)₅(NO₂)]²⁺ in which NO₂⁻ is bonded through the oxygen atom is called the ________ isomer.
explanation: When the NO₂⁻ ligand bonds through the oxygen atom (–ONO), the resulting complex is called the nitrito isomer (or nitrito-O isomer). This is in contrast to the nitro isomer, where NO₂⁻ bonds through the nitrogen atom (–NO₂). The two forms — [Co(NH₃)₅(NO₂)]²⁺ (nitro, N-bonded) and [Co(NH₃)₅(ONO)]²⁺ (nitrito, O-bonded) — are linkage isomers of each other. The nitrito isomer is generally less stable and can rearrange to the more stable nitro form.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: The nitro isomer is the one where NO₂⁻ bonds through the nitrogen atom (N-bonded), not through oxygen.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the nitro and nitrito naming conventions.

elimination_hints.hint: Nitrate refers to the NO₃⁻ ion, which is a different anion from NO₂⁻ and is not typically an ambidentate ligand.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing nitrite (NO₂⁻) with nitrate (NO₃⁻).

elimination_hints.hint: Nitroso refers to the –NO group, which is different from the nitrito (–ONO) bonding mode of the NO₂⁻ ligand.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the nomenclature of different nitrogen-oxygen containing groups.

question_text: A chemistry student prepares two samples of a cobalt coordination compound with the molecular formula CoCl₃·4NH₃. Sample X, when treated with excess AgNO₃, gives one mole of AgCl per mole of compound. Sample Y, when tested with the same reagent, gives two moles of AgCl per mole of compound. Based on this observation, what is the correct formulation of Sample Y?
explanation: The molecular formula CoCl₃·4NH₃ can be written in different ways depending on which Cl⁻ ions are inside or outside the coordination sphere. Sample X gives 1 mol AgCl, meaning it has one ionizable Cl⁻ outside the coordination sphere, so its formulation is [CoCl₂(NH₃)₄]Cl. Sample Y gives 2 mol AgCl, meaning it has two ionizable Cl⁻ ions outside, so its formulation is [CoCl(NH₃)₄]Cl₂. These two are ionization isomers of each other. Note: for Sample Y, only one Cl⁻ is in the coordination sphere as a ligand, while the other two are counter ions.
subject: chemistry

elimination_hints.hint: This formulation has all three Cl⁻ as ligands, so it would give 0 mol AgCl, not 2 mol.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not accounting for the coordination number and ignoring that NH₃ cannot be a counter ion.

elimination_hints.hint: This formulation has no Cl⁻ ligands and all three as counter ions, which would give 3 mol AgCl, not 2 mol. Also, Co³⁺ with only 4 NH₃ ligands would have coordination number 4, which is uncommon for Co³⁺.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not matching the AgCl precipitation data with the number of ionizable chloride ions.

elimination_hints.hint: This is the formulation of Sample X (1 ionizable Cl⁻ giving 1 mol AgCl), not Sample Y which produces 2 mol AgCl.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the two samples and their respective AgCl precipitation results.
